problem_summary: 2021 MCM Problem D (ICM, "music influence" / Integrative Collective Music Society). Note: the corpus tag labels this operations_research, but the actual problem is a network-science / data-analytics task on music evolution. Teams were given four CSVs (influence_data.csv follower-influencer edges; full_music_data.csv per-song features; data_by_artist.csv; data_by_year.csv) and asked to (1) build a directed influencer-follower network and quantify each influencer's influence, (2) define a music-similarity measure and compare within- vs between-genre similarity, (3) characterize and distinguish genres and their change over time, (4) determine whether influence actually drives similarity and find the most "contagious" musical characteristics, (5) identify revolutionary artists/characteristics, (6) build a dynamic measure of influencers and genre change over time, and (7) explain how social/technological events shape music. This O-award paper (#2100242) solves all seven sub-questions by composing four chained network-based models on top of a single artist-influence graph.
models_used: Influence Evaluation Model via a MODIFIED PageRank on the directed influencer->follower network. They invert standard PageRank: instead of importance flowing toward parent pages, PR flows from child (follower) nodes back up to parent (influencer) nodes, so an artist's PR aggregates the influence credited by everyone they influenced. Formula: PR(Vi) = (1-p)/N + p * sum over out-neighbors Vj of PR(Vj)/L(Vj), with damping p=0.85. Output: per-artist PR as an 'absolute influence' score, run on both the full network and per-genre subnetworks. The Beatles top both (PR~0.0116 overall, 0.035 in Pop/Rock).

Similarity Model Based on Distance of Vectors. Each artist is a feature vector over ~9-10 normalized audio features (danceability, energy, valence, tempo, loudness, mode, acousticness, instrumentalness, liveness, speechiness; key deliberately dropped on music-theory grounds). Two distance measures computed: Minkowski/Euclidean distance converted to similarity via sim = e^(-d), and cosine similarity. Rendered as artist x artist heatmaps sorted by genre to expose block-diagonal structure.

Hybrid Model of Influence and Similarity (genre generalization). Generalizes per-artist PR into genre-level influence indices: within-genre influence FI_k and cross-genre influence FO_{k1,k2}, by splitting each artist's followers into same-genre (a) vs other-genre (b_k) counts and weighting by PR. Generalizes similarity into within-genre similarity SI_k and between-genre SO_{k1,k2} as averaged pairwise sims. Both drawn as genre x genre heatmaps.

Inheriting Degree Model (the 'contagiousness' index). Defines a Boolean inheritance indicator G(k)=1 when feature k of an influencer and follower match within +/-5%, then IH(x_k) = (successful inheritances of feature k) / (total influence edges) per genre. Produces a 10-feature x 20-genre heatmap of how 'contagious' each musical characteristic is, with IH>=0.6 deemed strongly inherited.

Analysis Model of Music Evolution Process (Q5-7). Q5: track moderate-IH features (energy, tempo, acousticness, instrumentalness, speechiness) over time in data_by_year; a sharp mutation flags a revolution (1964-69 acousticness crash + energy rise -> Beatles identified, with 5 named songs). Q6: compare the genre-average feature curve E_ki against the most-popular-song-of-year curve P_ki; popular songs lead and the genre average lags, defining 'dynamic influencers'. Q7: PG(t) = sum of member-artist PR per genre per era, normalized and overlaid against a historical-events timeline (WWII, US civil-rights movement, Moore's law / PC adoption).
key_techniques: Single shared graph reused everywhere: one directed influence network underpins all four models, so PR computed once is recycled into genre influence, inheritance, and PG(t). This gives the whole paper a unified spine instead of seven disconnected ad-hoc answers.
Deliberate inversion of PageRank semantics (edge points influencer->follower, but PR propagates follower->influencer) to fit influence rather than web authority, with the choice explicitly justified.
Two independent similarity metrics (Euclidean-via-exponential and cosine) computed and shown to agree (symmetric, same block structure) as an internal cross-check of robustness.
Genre-level generalization by averaging artist-level quantities, with clean decomposition L(Vi)=a+b and b=b1+...+bk to separate within- vs cross-genre influence.
A crisp, falsifiable definition of 'contagiousness': +/-5% feature match -> Boolean inherited, ratio across all influence edges. Turns a vague prompt word into a computable index.

Using inheriting degree as a proxy for temporal stability: pick MODERATE-IH features for revolution detection (too-stable features never move; too-volatile features are noise) — a genuinely clever selection heuristic.
Lead-lag comparison of popular-song curve vs genre-average curve to operationalize 'dynamic influencer'.
Overlaying a normalized influence-sum curve PG(t) on a historical timeline to convert Q7 (a qualitative 'explain society's effect' prompt) into a visual quantitative argument.
Sensitivity analysis on the two structural knobs that actually matter: damping p in 0.85+/-0.15 (error <5%) and feature-vector dimension 10+/-3 (error <2%).
Model optimization section adding a time-decay/outcome factor P_i = a*W_i^b * e^(-c*dt) folded into PR for larger, longer-span datasets.
why_it_won: "From a judge's view this paper wins on coherence and completeness rather than mathematical sophistication. (1) It answers all seven sub-questions, and every answer is bound to one unifying object — the influence network — so the paper reads as a single integrated method, not seven mini-projects. (2) It takes a familiar, defensible workhorse (PageRank) and adapts it thoughtfully (inverting propagation direction) with explicit justification, which signals understanding rather than tool-grabbing. (3) Sanity checks are everywhere and convincing to a domain-aware judge: top-PR artists are 'legends of their genre' (Beatles, Bob Dylan, Chuck Berry, Elvis), genre influence/similarity heatmaps reproduce known lineages (Pop/Rock fed by Blues/Country/Folk/Jazz/R&B), and PG(t) tracks real history (WWII hitting classical, civil-rights boosting blues, Moore's law lifting electronic). Face-validity against known music history is the paper's strongest persuasion device. (4) It manufactures original, named, quantitative indices (FI, FO, SI, SO, IH, PG(t)) — judges reward teams that invent defensible metrics instead of only reporting library outputs. (5) Honest, well-targeted sensitivity analysis plus a scalability/optimization extension show engineering maturity. (6) Clean structure: per-model establishment-then-solution layout, an explicit assumptions list, a notation table, and a genuinely good one-page ICM Society memo that translates the math into plain claims."
section_highlights: "Summary clearly maps four models to the seven questions up front, so a judge knows the architecture in 30 seconds. Assumptions (only 4, each justified, e.g. equal influence weight because the data gives none; 5% inheritance threshold) keep the model honest and bounded. Notation table consolidates ~25 symbols before the math starts. Each model section follows a disciplined 'Establishment of Model -> Solution of Problem N' rhythm. Strong use of visuals as argument: genre-sorted similarity heatmaps whose block-diagonal red squares VISUALLY prove within-genre similarity > between-genre; the PG(t)-over-history overlay; per-feature inheritance heatmap. The Beatles recur as a narrative through-line (top influencer, top similarity cluster, revolutionary of the 1960s) giving the paper a memorable story. Sensitivity analysis isolates exactly the two parameters that matter. The closing one-page memo to the 'ICM Society' restates purpose/advantages/conclusions in accessible language — a direct, well-executed response to the prompt's communication requirement."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many figures in the markdown are clearly fabricated/placeholder grids (the heatmaps in Figs 7-16 show synthetic 0.95/0.85/0.75 gradient patterns and the genre-influence table is filled with constant 0.01-0.14 ramps), so the rendered numerics do not actually correspond to a real computation. Whether this is OCR/extraction corruption or the team mocking up figures is unclear, but a future team should NOT treat these tables as real results.

The +/-5% inheritance threshold is arbitrary and is applied as a percentage of x_ik, which behaves badly for features near zero or on different natural scales (e.g. tempo vs a 0-1 valence) — not dimensionally consistent.
Equal-influence assumption (every influencer affects each follower identically) is a strong simplification the data forced, but it flattens real differences in influence strength.

The modified PageRank is described somewhat ambiguously (text says child distributes PR to parents, equations mix in/out-degree notation L and F inconsistently); a reader cannot fully reproduce it from the prose alone.
Revolution detection (Q5) admittedly only catches a 'few particular super influencers' — the team flags this as a weakness; it cannot find subtler or distributed revolutions.
Q6 uses only the single most-popular song per year, explicitly ignoring the rest of the chart, biasing the dynamic-influence measure.
Genre-distinguishing claim ('use similarity for clustering, then label by inner-genre similarity') is asserted but never actually executed — no clustering algorithm or validation is shown.
No train/validation discipline or ground-truth comparison beyond eyeball face-validity; persuasiveness rests on the reader already knowing music history.
References are thin and several are loosely related (a Minkowski many-objective-optimization paper cited as the distance source).
reusable_patterns: One-graph-to-rule-them-all: build a single network artifact early, compute one node-importance score (PageRank), then derive every later answer (group-level indices, contagion, time series) from that same object. Gives a multi-part paper structural unity that judges reward.
Adapt a well-known algorithm with an explicitly justified twist (here: reverse PageRank propagation) instead of inventing from scratch or using it off-the-shelf — signals comprehension.
Cross-validate with two independent metrics (Euclidean+cosine) and show they agree as a cheap robustness argument.

Coin named, quantitative, defensible indices for every fuzzy prompt word ('influence' -> PR/FI/FO, 'similarity' -> SI/SO, 'contagious' -> IH, 'dynamic influencer' -> popular-vs-average lead/lag, 'social influence' -> PG(t) over a history timeline).
Genre/group generalization by averaging per-entity scores and decomposing edges into within-group vs cross-group counts (L = a + b, b = sum b_k).

Use a derived stability proxy to SELECT what to analyze (pick moderate-inheritance features for revolution detection) — a transferable trick: don't analyze everything, analyze the variables your model says are informative.

Validate against domain ground truth qualitatively: list top results and assert they match known reality (legendary artists, known genre lineages, real historical events) — powerful when a numeric ground truth is unavailable.
Targeted sensitivity analysis on only the few structural hyperparameters that matter (damping factor, feature dimension), reporting bounded error percentages.
Include an explicit 'optimization / scalability' section showing the model extends to bigger data (add time-decay factor to PR) — demonstrates forward-thinking maturity.
Disciplined repeating section template (Establishment of Model -> Solution of Problem N) plus assumptions list + notation table + plain-language stakeholder memo for the communication deliverable.
